quiz_questions.question_text: A refracting telescope uses _____ to gather and focus light.

options.text: Lenses
Mirrors
Prisms
Antennas

quiz_questions.explanation: Refracting telescopes use glass lenses to bend (refract) light to a focal point. They were the first type of telescope used in astronomy.

quiz_questions.question_text: A reflecting telescope uses _____ to gather light.

options.text: Mirrors
Lenses
Filters
Crystals

quiz_questions.explanation: Reflecting telescopes use curved mirrors (typically parabolic) to gather and focus light. Newton invented the first practical reflecting telescope.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which telescope was launched into space and orbits above Earth's atmosphere?

options.text: Hubble Space Telescope
Arecibo Telescope
Keck Telescope
Very Large Telescope

quiz_questions.explanation: The Hubble Space Telescope orbits above Earth's atmosphere, giving it sharp images free from atmospheric distortion. It was launched in 1990.

quiz_questions.question_text: Radio telescopes detect which type of electromagnetic radiation?

options.text: Radio waves
Visible light
X-rays
Gamma rays

quiz_questions.explanation: Radio telescopes use large dish antennas to detect radio waves from space. They can operate day or night and through clouds, unlike optical telescopes.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is one advantage of space-based telescopes over ground-based ones?

options.text: They avoid distortion from Earth's atmosphere
They are cheaper to build
They are easier to repair
They are closer to the stars

quiz_questions.explanation: Earth's atmosphere blurs and absorbs certain wavelengths of light. Space telescopes like Hubble and JWST avoid this problem entirely, producing much sharper images.

quiz_questions.question_text: The James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) primarily observes in which part of the electromagnetic spectrum?

options.text: Infrared
Ultraviolet
Radio
Gamma ray

quiz_questions.explanation: JWST is optimized for infrared observations, allowing it to see through dust clouds and observe the earliest galaxies formed after the Big Bang.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which space probe has traveled beyond our solar system into interstellar space?

options.text: Voyager 1
Mars Rover Curiosity
Hubble Space Telescope
International Space Station

quiz_questions.explanation: Voyager 1, launched in 1977, became the first human-made object to enter interstellar space in 2012, after passing beyond the heliosphere.

quiz_questions.question_text: A common problem with large refracting telescopes is:

options.text: Chromatic aberration — different colors focus at different points
They cannot be pointed at the sky
They can only see in infrared light
They require nuclear power

quiz_questions.explanation: Chromatic aberration occurs because glass lenses bend different wavelengths of light by different amounts, causing color fringing. This limits the practical size of refracting telescopes.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Chandra space telescope is designed to detect:

options.text: X-rays
Radio waves
Visible light
Sound waves

quiz_questions.explanation: The Chandra X-ray Observatory detects X-ray emissions from hot, energetic regions of the universe such as exploded stars, galaxy clusters, and matter around black holes.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does a larger telescope aperture (opening) allow?

options.text: It collects more light, showing fainter objects
It makes the telescope lighter
It blocks more starlight
It reduces the field of view

quiz_questions.explanation: A larger aperture gathers more photons, allowing the telescope to detect fainter objects and resolve finer details. This is why modern telescopes have mirrors many meters across.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'aperture' in the context of a telescope?

options.text: The diameter of the main lens or mirror that gathers light
The length of the telescope tube
The type of camera attached to the telescope
The brightness of the object being observed

quiz_questions.explanation: Aperture is the diameter of a telescope's primary light-gathering element (lens or mirror). A larger aperture means more light-gathering power and better resolution.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'chromatic aberration'?

options.text: A defect where a lens focuses different colors of light at different points
A type of mirror used in reflecting telescopes
The twinkling of stars caused by the atmosphere
The redshift of distant galaxies

quiz_questions.explanation: Chromatic aberration occurs because glass refracts (bends) different wavelengths by different amounts. Blue light bends more than red, creating color fringes. Reflecting telescopes avoid this problem because mirrors reflect all colors equally.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'electromagnetic spectrum'?

options.text: The full range of electromagnetic radiation, from radio waves to gamma rays
The visible colors of a rainbow only
The sound waves produced by stars
The gravitational waves from black holes

quiz_questions.explanation: The electromagnetic spectrum spans all types of electromagnetic radiation: radio, microwave, infrared, visible, ultraviolet, X-ray, and gamma ray. Different telescopes observe different parts of this spectrum.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'radio telescope'?

options.text: A telescope that detects radio-wavelength radiation from space using a dish antenna
A telescope that plays radio music while observing
A telescope that can only see planets
A handheld device for listening to space sounds

quiz_questions.explanation: Radio telescopes use large dish antennas to collect radio waves emitted by celestial objects. The Arecibo dish (305 m) and FAST (500 m) are famous examples.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'light-gathering power'?

options.text: A telescope's ability to collect photons, determined by its aperture size
The speed at which a telescope can rotate
The amount of electricity a telescope requires
The weight of the telescope's mirrors

quiz_questions.explanation: Light-gathering power is proportional to the area of the aperture (πr²). Doubling the aperture diameter quadruples the light-gathering power, allowing detection of much fainter objects.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'atmospheric distortion' (also called 'seeing')?

options.text: The blurring of images caused by turbulence in Earth's atmosphere
The bending of light by a black hole
The absorption of light by interstellar dust
The reflection of light off the Moon's surface

quiz_questions.explanation: Atmospheric distortion (seeing) makes stars appear to twinkle and blurs telescope images. It is caused by pockets of air at different temperatures refracting light. Space telescopes and adaptive optics overcome this problem.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'spectroscopy'?

options.text: The technique of splitting light into its component wavelengths to analyze composition and motion
The technique of taking photographs of space
The technique of measuring the weight of stars
The technique of counting stars in a galaxy

quiz_questions.explanation: Spectroscopy splits light into a spectrum, revealing absorption and emission lines that identify chemical elements, temperature, density, and radial velocity (via Doppler shift) of celestial objects.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'space probe'?

options.text: An unmanned spacecraft sent to explore celestial bodies and collect data
A manned spacecraft that orbits Earth
A type of antenna used for radio telescopes
A handheld tool used by astronauts on spacewalks

quiz_questions.explanation: Space probes are robotic vehicles sent to other planets, moons, asteroids, or beyond the solar system. Examples include Voyager, New Horizons (Pluto flyby), and the Mars rovers.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'resolution' mean for a telescope?

options.text: The ability to distinguish fine details or separate closely spaced objects
The total weight of the telescope
The number of colors a telescope can see
The size of the telescope building

quiz_questions.explanation: Resolution (angular resolution) is how well a telescope can distinguish two closely spaced objects. Higher resolution means sharper, more detailed images. It improves with larger aperture and shorter wavelength.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'Lagrange point' where JWST orbits?

options.text: A point in space where the gravitational pull of two bodies balances, allowing stable orbits
The closest point of a planet's orbit to the Sun
The center of the Milky Way galaxy
The edge of the observable universe

quiz_questions.explanation: JWST orbits at the Sun-Earth L2 Lagrange point, about 1.5 million km from Earth. At L2, the combined gravity of the Sun and Earth allows JWST to orbit the Sun in sync with Earth while staying in Earth's shadow.

quiz_questions.question_text: An astronomy class compares images of the same nebula taken by Hubble (visible light) and Spitzer (infrared). The infrared image reveals stars hidden by dust. Why?

options.text: Infrared light can pass through dust clouds that block visible light
Spitzer is simply a more powerful telescope than Hubble
Infrared cameras use better film than visible-light cameras
The dust only exists in the visible-light image

quiz_questions.explanation: Dust grains absorb and scatter visible light but allow longer-wavelength infrared light to pass through. This is why infrared telescopes like Spitzer and JWST can reveal objects hidden behind dust clouds.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student notices that stars twinkle when seen from the ground but not from photos taken by Hubble. What explains the difference?

options.text: Hubble is above the atmosphere, so atmospheric turbulence doesn't blur its images
Hubble uses a special anti-twinkle filter
Stars only twinkle at night, and Hubble only observes during the day
Ground-based telescopes are always broken

quiz_questions.explanation: Twinkling (scintillation) is caused by turbulence in Earth's atmosphere bending starlight. Hubble orbits above the atmosphere at about 540 km altitude, so it sees steady, unblurred starlight.

quiz_questions.question_text: A radio astronomer can observe a distant galaxy on a cloudy night, but an optical astronomer cannot. Why?

options.text: Radio waves pass through clouds and atmosphere; visible light is blocked by clouds
Radio telescopes are more expensive and therefore more powerful
Visible light doesn't travel as far as radio waves
The optical astronomer forgot to open the dome

quiz_questions.explanation: Radio waves have much longer wavelengths than visible light and pass through clouds, dust, and even some solid objects. This makes radio telescopes uniquely valuable for observing through Earth's atmosphere.

quiz_questions.question_text: NASA plans to build a new space telescope. Engineers debate between a single 10-meter mirror and a segmented mirror of 25 smaller hexagonal pieces forming a 6.5-meter aperture. What is the main advantage of the segmented design?

options.text: A segmented mirror can be folded for launch and deployed in space, solving rocket size constraints
Segmented mirrors are always cheaper than single mirrors
Segmented mirrors produce better color images
A single 10-meter mirror would be too heavy for gravity on Earth

quiz_questions.explanation: JWST uses a 6.5-meter segmented mirror made of 18 hexagonal gold-coated beryllium segments. It was folded to fit inside the rocket and unfolded after launch — a revolutionary engineering solution that allowed a much larger mirror than any single-piece mirror that could fit in a rocket fairing.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student says: 'We should build all our telescopes in space since they're better.' What is a valid counterargument?

options.text: Space telescopes cost much more and are much harder to maintain or repair than ground-based ones
Ground-based telescopes actually produce better images than space telescopes
There is no way to launch a telescope into space
Space telescopes can only observe one object their entire lifetime

quiz_questions.explanation: While space telescopes avoid atmospheric distortion, they cost billions of dollars and are extremely difficult to repair. Ground-based telescopes with adaptive optics can approach space-quality images at a fraction of the cost, making both types essential.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Arecibo radio telescope collapsed in 2020. Scientists said there is no direct replacement for certain observations it could make. What unique feature made Arecibo special?

options.text: Its 305-meter dish gave it unmatched sensitivity for radar astronomy and detecting faint signals
It was the only telescope that could see visible light
It was the only telescope in the southern hemisphere
It was the only telescope powered by solar energy

quiz_questions.explanation: Arecibo's 305-meter (1,000-foot) dish was one of the largest single-aperture radio telescopes ever built. Its huge collecting area made it ideal for planetary radar, pulsar timing, and searches for extraterrestrial intelligence (SETI).

quiz_questions.question_text: A team discovers a new exoplanet by noticing periodic dips in a star's brightness. What observational technique are they using?

options.text: The transit method — the planet blocks some starlight as it passes in front of the star
Spectroscopy — they split the star's light into colors
Radio astronomy — they detected radio waves from the planet
Astrometry — they measured the star's position shifting

quiz_questions.explanation: The transit method detects exoplanets by measuring the tiny decrease in a star's brightness when a planet crosses in front of it. NASA's Kepler and TESS missions have discovered thousands of exoplanets this way.

quiz_questions.question_text: JWST orbits at the L2 Lagrange point, 1.5 million km from Earth. If something breaks, astronauts cannot visit to fix it (unlike Hubble). Why was this location chosen despite the risk?

options.text: L2 keeps JWST in Earth's shadow, keeping it cold enough for infrared observations while maintaining a stable orbit
L2 is the closest point to the nearest star
L2 has strong gravity that protects the telescope from space debris
L2 was the only location NASA had permission to use

quiz_questions.explanation: JWST's infrared detectors must be kept extremely cold (below 50 K). At L2, the sunshield always faces the Sun and Earth, keeping the telescope in permanent shadow. The gravitational balance at L2 means minimal fuel is needed to maintain orbit.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two observatories observe the same supernova remnant — one using an X-ray telescope and one using a visible-light telescope. They see different structures. Why is multi-wavelength astronomy important?

options.text: Different wavelengths reveal different physical processes and structures that no single wavelength shows completely
One of the telescopes must be malfunctioning
X-ray telescopes always see more detail than visible-light ones
The supernova looks different because the observations were taken at different times

quiz_questions.explanation: X-rays reveal extremely hot gas (millions of degrees) while visible light shows cooler gas and stars. By combining data across the spectrum, astronomers build a complete picture of cosmic objects — something impossible with any single wavelength.

quiz_questions.question_text: A school has $50,000 to buy a telescope. An 8-inch reflector costs $2,000 while an 8-inch refractor costs $8,000. A teacher recommends the reflector. What is the BEST scientific reason?

options.text: Reflecting telescopes avoid chromatic aberration and are more cost-effective at larger apertures
Reflecting telescopes are always more accurate than refractors
Refractors cannot be used for stargazing
The reflector weighs less because it uses no glass

quiz_questions.explanation: Reflecting telescopes use mirrors that reflect all wavelengths equally (no chromatic aberration), and mirrors are easier and cheaper to manufacture at large sizes than lenses. For the same aperture, reflectors offer better value.
